articles.title: Iran Says US Deal Not Imminent | The Pulse 5/25/2026
articles.summary:
Iran has denied that a deal to end the war with the US is imminent, after US officials said they were closing in on a deal. Secretary of State Marco Rubio said an agreement could come as soon as Monday, but Iran’s Foreign Ministry Spokesman Esmail Baghaei said “It is true that a consensus was reached on many of the topics discussed, but no one can claim that the signing of an agreement is imminent.”

Turkish police entered the headquarters of the main opposition CHP party following a court decision last week, that removed Ozgur Ozel as its leader. The move deepens a political crisis that saw a market selloff last week.

And Huawei says it's made a breakthrough in making advanced semiconductors without cutting-edge equipment, potentially reducing the gap with industry leader TSMC.

Today's guests: Marco Valli, UniCredit, Chief European Economist & Alex Karnal, Braidwell, Co-Founder and CIO. (Source: Bloomberg)

articles.title: Italy Tightens Grip on Payments Company Nexi With Stake Increase
articles.summary: Italy’s state lender Cassa Depositi e Prestiti SpA is set to increase its stake in Nexi SpA, tightening its grip on the payment company considered strategic by Prime Minister Giorgia Meloni’s government.

articles.title: SoftBank Taps Retail Market Again With $1.6 Billion Yen Bond

articles.title: Iran Says US Deal Not Imminent | The Opening Trade 5/25/2026
articles.summary:
Iran's Foreign Ministry spokesman says a deal to open the Strait of Hormuz is not imminent, but that consensus has been reached on many issues. Earlier, oil fell after senior US officials said Washington and Tehran were closing in on an agreement to open the vital waterway.

The Opening Trade has everything you need to know as markets open across Europe. With analysis you won't find anywhere else, we break down the biggest stories of the day and speak to top guests who have skin in the game. Hosted by Joumanna Bercetche and Tom Mackenzie. (Source: Bloomberg)

articles.title: What It Takes to Run One of London's Most Popular Pubs | Odd Lots
articles.summary: Restaurants are great microcosms for macro-economic trends. They sit at the intersection of everything from consumer confidence to commodity costs to the labor market. According to the British Beer and Pub Association, approximately two pubs a day have closed in England during the first quarter of 2026. Could pubs tell us something about larger trends in the British economy? We talk to Oisin Rogers and Ashley Palmer-Watts, co-founders of The Devonshire, a famed London pub, top find out. Rogers, the publican, discusses the difference between a good and bad pub, why he hates the word 'gastropub,' and how the indoor smoking ban changed the meaning of pubs for the average Londoner. In a segment from our London live show, chef Palmer-Watts, details the factors that lead to a perfect pint of Guinness, why higher ingredient costs does not always correlate to higher menu prices, and about making a Victorian-era meat fruit for Apple's Jony Ive. (Source: Bloomberg)
